title: Design a Real-Time Integration Hub
description: 10M+ messages/day, 500+ flows, 99.95% SLA, <100ms P99 latency. Event Mesh vs Kafka vs Pulsar comparison, Schema Registry, routing/partitioning, connector architecture (SAP outbound/inbound), monitoring (consumer lag, throughput), exactly-once semantics.

sections.title: Requirements & Scale

description.value:
A real-time integration hub is the central nervous system connecting SAP systems with hundreds of internal and external applications. This design question tests your understanding of messaging systems, event-driven architecture, exactly-once semantics, and operational excellence at scale. FAANG interviews for enterprise-focused roles frequently ask this variant.

Functional Requirements: (1) Support 500+ integration flows connecting SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Ariba, and 200+ non-SAP applications (Salesforce, Workday, custom microservices). (2) Handle multiple integration patterns: publish-subscribe for events, point-to-point for requests, and streaming for CDC (Change Data Capture) from SAP tables. (3) Message routing based on content (header-based, property-based), with support for transformations (XML to JSON, IDoc to CloudEvents). (4) Exactly-once delivery for financial transactions, at-least-once for operational data.

Non-Functional Requirements: (1) Throughput: 10 million+ messages/day peak (approximately 115+ messages/second average, 500+ messages/second at peak). (2) Latency: P99 under 100ms end-to-end (from SAP system producing to consumer receiving). (3) Availability: 99.95% uptime (approximately 4.4 hours downtime/year), with particular focus on financial close periods where downtime is unacceptable. (4) Durability: messages persisted durably with zero data loss tolerance for financial transactions. (5) Ordering: messages from a single SAP document (IDoc) must be processed in order within a partition.

The integration hub replaces a legacy SAP PI/PO (Process Integration/Process Orchestration) system that handles 300+ interfaces but has 30% failure rate and no centralized observability. The new hub must support a migration plan where legacy PI/PO flows are migrated incrementally over 12 months.

sections.codeBlock:
" Throughput calculation at 10M msg/day peak
" Rate: 10,000,000 / 86,400 ≈ 116 msg/s avg
" Peak: 500 msg/s (5x multiplier)"
" Partition sizing:" partitions = (peak_mbps / 10 MB/s) × RF × 1.5
" Example: sap.fi.invoice = 50 MB/s peak
"   partitions = (50/10) × 3 × 1.5 ≈ 23 partitions"
" Key design decisions:
" - Topic per domain: sap.fi.*, sap.sd.*, sap.mm.*
" - Partition key = document ID for ordered processing
" - Replication factor = 3 for durability (99.95% SLA)" Consumer lag alerting:
"   Warning:  >1,000 msg lag (~10s backlog)
"   Critical: >10,000 msg (~2 min backlog)
"   Page:     >100,000 msg (~20 min, consumer likely dead)

decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is this integration point-to-point or publish-subscribe?" Point-to-point integrations (SAP-to-SAP with transactional semantics) use the hub/spoke model with guaranteed delivery. Publish-subscribe (one event, N consumers) uses the event mesh with topics and subscriptions.

branches.condition: SAP-to-SAP integration with transactional consistency (IDoc, RFC, BAPI)
branches.path: Hub/Spoke with guaranteed delivery. The hub acts as the central router connecting spoke SAP systems. Each spoke communicates only with the hub, not peer-to-peer. Enables monitoring, throttling, and dead-letter queues per spoke.

branches.condition: SAP-to-non-SAP with multiple consumers per event
branches.path: Publish-subscribe via event mesh. One event from SAP S/4HANA published to a topic, consumed by analytics, data warehouse, and CRM independently. Each consumer manages its own offset.

branches.condition: Real-time CDC from SAP tables to multiple data stores
branches.path: Streaming hub with Kafka Connect. SAP CDC connector captures table changes, publishes to Kafka topic, multiple sink connectors (S3, Elasticsearch, Snowflake) consume independently.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The hub/spoke vs event mesh decision is not binary. At Staff+, propose a hybrid: hub/spoke for transactional SAP-to-SAP flows (where exactly-once delivery and ordering matter), event mesh for analytics and streaming use cases (where multiple consumers and independent offsets matter). The integration hub architecture should support both patterns.

sections.title: Event Mesh vs Kafka vs Pulsar

description.value:
The choice of messaging backbone is the most consequential architectural decision for an integration hub. We evaluate three options: SAP BTP Event Mesh (managed Kafka), Apache Kafka (self-managed or Confluent Cloud), and Apache Pulsar. Each has strengths and weaknesses for the SAP integration context.

SAP BTP Event Mesh (Managed Kafka): Event Mesh is SAP's managed Kafka offering integrated with BTP. It provides native SAP protocols (CloudEvents, AMQP, MQTT), direct integration with SAP applications via BTP destinations, and managed Kafka clusters with SLAs. Pros: Deep SAP integration (Connectivity Service, Destination Service, Principal Propagation), CloudEvents-compliant by default, managed operations (no Kafka expertise needed for the team), and built-in monitoring via SAP Cloud ALM. Cons: Higher per-message cost than raw Kafka, limited control over cluster configuration (partition count, retention policy), vendor lock-in to SAP ecosystem, and less mature ecosystem (no Kafka Connect, fewer stream processing options).

Apache Kafka (Self-Managed or Confluent): Kafka is the industry-standard event streaming platform. Pros: Battle-tested at massive scale (LinkedIn, Netflix, Uber), rich ecosystem (Kafka Connect, Kafka Streams, ksqlDB), flexible configuration, open-source foundation. Cons: Requires significant operational expertise (Kafka is notoriously complex to operate), lacks native SAP protocol support (requires custom adapters), and self-managed means the team must handle broker upgrades, rebalancing, and disaster recovery.

Apache Pulsar: Pulsar is a newer entrant with a two-layer architecture (serving layer + bookie storage layer). Pros: Native multi-tenancy, geo-replication built-in, decoupled compute/storage (independent scaling), and lower per-broker overhead. Cons: Smaller ecosystem and community, fewer enterprise SAP integrations, and less operational tooling maturity.

Recommendation: For a SAP-centric integration hub, start with Event Mesh for SAP-to-SAP and SAP-to-BTP integrations (leverage native protocols), use Kafka (Confluent Cloud) for non-SAP integrations and high-throughput streaming (CDC, analytics), and bridge both via a Federation Layer that routes messages between Event Mesh and Kafka based on content type and destination.

sections.codeBlock:
" Kafka producer config (pseudocode)
producer = KafkaProducer({
  bootstrap_servers: "kafka-cluster:9092,kafka-cluster:9093"
  acks: "all"  # Wait for all replicas
  compression_type: "snappy"
  batch_size: 16384  # 16KB batches
  linger_ms: 5  # 5ms max wait before sending batch
  enable_idempotence: true
})

# Publish SAP sales order event
data = {
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "type": "sap.s4.beh.salesorder.v2.Changed",
  "source": "/sap/s4/salesorder",
  "id": uuid(),
  "time": datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
  "datacontenttype": "application/json",
  "data": {
    "salesOrderId": "1001",
    "customerId": "CUST001",
    "totalAmount": 2500.00,
    "currency": "USD",
    "status": "APPROVED"
  }
}

producer.send("sap.sd.salesorder", key="1001", value=json.dumps(data))
producer.flush()  # Ensure delivery

# Consumer
consumer = KafkaConsumer(
  "sap.sd.salesorder",
  bootstrap_servers="kafka-cluster:9092",
  group_id="salesorder-processor",
  enable_auto_commit=false,
  auto_offset_reset="earliest"
)

for msg in consumer:
  process(msg.value)
  consumer.commit()  # Manual offset commit

calculation.formula: TCO = ClusterCost x 12 + OpsFTE x Salary + PerMsgCost x Volume x 365

ap.monthlyPenalty: $14,200 self-managed Kafka: $4,200 cluster (3 x m5.2xlarge) + $10,000 (2 FTE x $5K ops cost) = $14,200/mo

calculation.breakeven: At 10M msg/day, Event Mesh saves 31% on TCO due to zero operational overhead. At 100M+ msg/day, self-managed Kafka becomes cheaper because per-message costs in Event Mesh grow linearly while Kafka cluster costs grow sub-linearly.

sections.title: Schema Registry & Message Evolution

description.value:
A schema registry is essential for a system with 500+ integration flows and long-lived events. SAP IDocs have implicit schemas (the IDoc type definition in the SAP system), but for event-driven integration, we need explicit, versioned, and validated schemas that live outside the SAP system.

Schema Format: We use CloudEvents as the standard envelope format (spec: specversion, type, source, id, time, datacontenttype, data). The payload inside data follows an Avro schema registered in the schema registry. Avro is chosen over Protobuf and JSON Schema because: (1) native support for schema evolution (reader/writer schemas), (2) compact binary serialization (30–50% smaller than JSON), (3) well-supported by Kafka ecosystem (Kafka Avro Serde, Kafka Connect Avro Converter).

Schema Evolution Rules: The schema registry enforces backward-compatible evolution: (1) Only ADD optional fields (never remove or rename), (2) Defaults must be specified for new fields, (3) Field types can only be widened (int → long, never long → int), (4) Enum types can only be extended (never remove values). Breaking changes require a new major version. Each message in the topic carries both the schema version (in the header) and the serialized payload. Consumers use the schema version to deserialize — a consumer built against schema v1 can still read messages produced by schema v2 (unknown fields are skipped).

IDoc Integration with Schema Registry: SAP IDocs are defined in transaction WE30 (IDoc type) and WE31 (segment type). The integration hub automatically generates an Avro schema from the IDoc type definition during onboarding. The schema generation maps: IDoc segments → Avro records, segment fields → Avro fields (with the same data types), and IDoc hierarchies (parent-child) → nested Avro records. The generated schema is published to the schema registry with metadata linking it back to the source IDoc type. This enables non-SAP consumers to process SAP data without any SAP middleware.

organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: Migrate from legacy SAP PI/PO using the Strangler Fig pattern: wrap existing PI/PO interfaces with the new schema registry as a proxy layer. Phase 1 (months 1-3): generate Avro schemas from all active IDoc types and deploy the schema registry alongside PI/PO — no existing interfaces are disrupted. Phase 2 (months 4-8): migrate high-volume interfaces to native CloudEvents one at a time. Phase 3 (months 9-12): sunset PI/PO after all 300+ interfaces are migrated.

stakeholders.concern: Schema registry adds middleware beyond our control. Our integration flows are tightly coupled to PI/PO routing logic.
stakeholders.address: The schema registry is additive, not a replacement. PI/PO still routes during Phases 1 and 2. We provide a PI/PO adapter converting PI/PO messages to CloudEvents with Avro schemas — no changes to PI/PO configuration required.

stakeholders.concern: Avro schemas are new to us. We use JSON and REST today.
stakeholders.address: The schema registry supports multiple serialization formats: Avro for high-throughput flows, JSON Schema for RESTful integrations, and Protobuf for gRPC services. Teams choose their format. Backward-compatibility validation runs regardless of format.

stakeholders.concern: Schema governance across 500+ flows needs clear ownership and change management.
stakeholders.address: Each schema has a designated domain owner (FI team owns sap.fi.* schemas). Schema changes go through CI validation for backward compatibility. Breaking changes require a new major version with 2-week notice period. Automated impact analysis shows affected consumers.

sections.title: Routing & Partitioning Strategy

description.value:
With 500+ integration flows across 10M+ daily messages, intelligent routing and partitioning is critical for performance, ordering guarantees, and operational manageability.

Topic Design: Topics are organized by Domain + Message Category. Examples: sap.fi.invoice, sap.sd.order, sap.mm.goods-movement, non-sap.salesforce.account. This domain-based isolation provides several benefits: (1) independent scaling (each topic can have different partition counts based on throughput), (2) clear ownership (the FI team owns sap.fi.* topics), (3) targeted monitoring (alert on consumer lag per domain), and (4) fine-grained security (RBAC per topic).

Partition Key Selection: The partition key determines ordering guarantees. For SAP messages that need ordered processing (e.g., all updates to a single purchase order must be processed in sequence), the partition key is the document number (e.g., Purchase Order ID, Invoice Number). For messages where ordering doesn't matter (e.g., notification events), a random partition key distributes load evenly. The partition key strategy must be documented per flow in the integration registry.

Content-Based Routing: The integration hub uses a router microservice that inspects message headers and content to determine the destination topic. The routing rules are configurable via a UI (no code changes) and support: header-based routing (message type, source system), content-based routing (company code > 1000 → topic A, else topic B), SLA-based routing (priority messages → dedicated high-throughput topic), and metadata-based routing (compliance-tagged messages → audited topic).

Partition Count Sizing: The partition count per topic is calculated based on throughput × retention. Formula: partitions = (peak_throughput_mbps / 10 MB/s) × replication_factor × 1.5. For example, a topic with 50 MB/s peak throughput and 3x replication: partitions = (50/10) × 3 × 1.5 = 22.5 ≈ 24 partitions. The 1.5x multiplier provides headroom for traffic spikes and rebalancing. Partition count is oversized upfront because changing partition count after data is produced is difficult (Kafka does not support partition splitting).

sections.codeBlock:
" Partition routing logic (pseudocode)
def route_message(msg):
    # Extract partition key from message
    doc_id = msg["data"]["salesOrderId"]
    domain = msg["type"].split(".")[1]  # e.g., "sd"

    # Ordered processing: hash doc_id to partition
    partition = hash(doc_id) % NUM_PARTITIONS

    # Content-based routing
    if msg["data"]["amount"] > 10000:
        topic = "sap.sd.highvalue"
    else:
        topic = "sap.sd.standard"

    # SLA-based: priority messages to dedicated topic
    if msg["headers"]["priority"] == "P1":
        topic = "sap.sd.priority"

    return (topic, partition)

" Topic naming convention: {source}.{domain}.{entity}.{version}
" Examples:
"   sap.fi.invoice.v1
"   sap.sd.order.v2
"   non-sap.salesforce.account.v1
" 
" Partition key = document ID (for ordered processing)
" Key schema: {company_code}-{document_type}-{document_number}
" Note: max 100 partitions per topic, 1000 topics per cluster

decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does the consumer need synchronous confirmation or async delivery?" Synchronous consumers (SAP BAPI/RFC calls) need request-reply semantics via the API Gateway. Async consumers (analytics, data warehouses) can use pub-sub with the event mesh.

branches.condition: Client needs immediate response (user-facing API, synchronous BAPI call)
branches.path: API Gateway with request-reply pattern. The gateway receives the request, publishes to a command topic, the consumer processes and publishes the response to a reply topic, the gateway returns the response to the caller. Timeout: 30s. Circuit breaker on 50% error rate.

branches.condition: Fire-and-forget delivery, no response expected (event notifications, CDC)
branches.path: Pub-sub via event mesh. Producer publishes to topic, multiple consumers receive independently. No API Gateway overhead — direct Kafka producer/consumer. Lower latency (2ms vs 15ms through gateway).

branches.condition: Mixed: some flows need sync, most are async
branches.path: Hybrid: API Gateway for synchronous request-reply flows (< 20% of traffic), event mesh for async flows (> 80%). The gateway routes internally to the same event mesh topics for ordered processing.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: At Staff+, do not propose an API Gateway for everything. Gateway adds 10-15ms of latency per request and is an additional operational burden. Use it only for the 20% of flows that need synchronous semantics. The other 80% go directly to the event mesh. This saves $5K/month in gateway infrastructure and reduces p99 latency by 10ms.

cp.monthlyPenalty: $4,500 API Gateway: 3 x $1,200 (c5n.xlarge) + $900 data transfer + $0 ops overhead for 5K req/s

ap.monthlyPenalty: $1,800 Direct pub-sub: zero gateway cost, just Kafka producer throughput at $0.00001/msg

calculation.breakeven: At 5K req/s, gateway costs 2.5x more than direct pub-sub but enables auth, rate limiting, and protocol translation. At 20K+ req/s, gateway cost becomes prohibitive and most flows should migrate to async pub-sub.

sections.title: Connector Architecture — SAP Outbound/Inbound

description.value:
The connector layer bridges SAP systems (which speak RFC, IDoc, SOAP, BAPI) and the event-driven hub (which speaks CloudEvents). This is the hardest engineering challenge because SAP protocols were designed before event-driven architecture existed. The connector architecture must handle protocol translation, credential management, backpressure, and fault isolation.

Connector Types: Three categories of connectors. SAP Outbound Connectors capture events from SAP systems. For IDocs: use ALE with Change Pointers (BDCP) — when an SAP document changes, a change pointer is created, picked up by a background job (RBDMIDOC), and sent to the integration hub via RFC-to-Event Mesh bridge. For CDS/CDT: use the SAP CDC service on BTP that streams table changes via Kafka. For BAPI/RFC: wrap synchronous BAPI calls in an async adapter that produces request events and consumes response events. SAP Inbound Connectors deliver events to SAP: receive CloudEvents, map to BAPI/RFC/IDoc, invoke the SAP system, and handle responses. Non-SAP Connectors for platforms like Salesforce, Workday, and custom apps use standard Kafka Connect with pre-built or custom connectors.

Connector Lifecycle: Each connector goes through a standardized lifecycle: Onboard (register connector type, configure connection parameters, test connectivity), Activate (start consuming/producing messages), Monitor (track throughput, error rate, latency), Decommission (drain messages, stop connector, archive offset). The lifecycle is managed via the Connector Registry — a central database of all connectors with their configuration, status, and health metrics.

Fault Isolation: Each connector runs in its own container (or separate thread pool) with resource limits (CPU, memory, max connections). A failing connector cannot consume resources from other connectors. Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures: if an SAP system is down, the connector trips after 5 consecutive failures, stops consuming from the topic, and alerts. The connector automatically reconnects after a configurable timeout (exponential backoff: 30s, 2min, 10min, 1hr).

sections.title: Monitoring — Consumer Lag, Throughput, SLA Compliance

description.value:
Monitoring an integration hub with 500+ flows requires a systematic approach to observability. You cannot debug a failed integration by SSHing into a broker. FAANG-style observability means: dashboards for every SLA dimension, automated alerting with runbooks, and self-service debugging tools for integration owners.

Four Golden Signals: For each integration flow, we monitor: Latency (P50/P99/P999 end-to-end from SAP produce to consumer ack), Throughput (messages/second, bytes/second, with trend lines), Error Rate (failed messages / total messages, broken down by error category: schema validation, transformation, delivery), and Consumer Lag (how far behind consumers are from the producer offset). Consumer lag is the most important metric — if lag grows unbounded, the system is falling behind and messages will be lost when retention limits are hit.

Consumer Lag Monitoring: Lag is monitored per consumer group per partition. Alert thresholds: Warning at lag > 1,000 messages (approximately 10 seconds of backlog at 100 msg/s), Critical at lag > 10,000 messages (approximately 2 minutes), and Page at lag > 100,000 (approximately 20 minutes, indicates consumer failure). The lag dashboard also shows: consumer group owner, consumer status (running/stopped/dead), last committed offset, and trend (increasing, stable, decreasing).

SLA Compliance Dashboard: A real-time dashboard tracks SLA attainment per integration flow. Green: within SLA (99.95% of messages delivered within 100ms), Yellow: approaching SLA breach (99.90% within limit), Red: SLA breached (<99.95%). The dashboard rolls up by domain (FI, SD, MM), by criticality (P1/P2/P3), and by counterparty (SAP vs non-SAP). SLA breaches trigger automated post-mortems: the system captures the time window, affected messages, root cause analysis, and remediation steps.

Tracing: Every message carries a unique trace ID (correlation ID) that is propagated from the source SAP system through the integration hub to the final consumer. The trace ID enables end-to-end distributed tracing across systems that don't share a common tracing infrastructure. The tracing dashboard shows: message flow path, timestamps at each hop, transformation applied, and final delivery status. This is essential for debugging the question every integration team hates: "Did my message get through?"

sections.codeBlock:
" Monitoring metrics per integration flow
" Prometheus-style metric definitions:
"
" # HELP integration_lag_seconds Consumer lag per partition
" # TYPE integration_lag_seconds gauge
" integration_lag_seconds{topic="sap.sd.salesorder",partition="0",consumer_group="order-proc"} 42.0
"
" # HELP integration_throughput Messages per second per topic
" # TYPE integration_throughput gauge
" integration_throughput{topic="sap.sd.salesorder"} 115.0
"
" # HELP integration_error_rate Error ratio per flow
" # TYPE integration_error_rate gauge
" integration_error_rate{flow="salesorder-inbound",category="schema"} 0.001
"
" Alert rules:
" lag > 10000 AND rate(lag[5m]) > 0  -> Critical (consumer falling behind)
" error_rate > 0.01  -> Warning (check DLQ)
" throughput < 10 AND expected > 50 -> Warning (source may be down)

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your 99.95% SLA means 4.4 hours of downtime per year. Our SAP financial close cannot tolerate any downtime during month-end. Why not design for 99.99%?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "99.99% would cost 3x more. 99.95% is the industry standard for enterprise integration hubs." (dismissive, doesn't answer the business constraint)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You are right to push on this. Let me address it by separating the SLA per business context. Our 99.95% target is the aggregate SLA. During financial close periods (12 hours per month-end, 144 hours per year), we operate under a 'Close Mode' that elevates the SLA to 99.99% using three mechanisms: (1) redundant active-active brokers in the close period — we spin up an additional Kafka broker in a different AZ, making the cluster 4-node instead of 3-node, which survives 2 simultaneous failures instead of 1, (2) priority queuing — close-related messages (FI/CO topics) get dedicated partitions with higher replication factor and guaranteed consumer capacity, (3) automated failover with 30-second RTO for close-critical flows — normally our failover RTO is 5 minutes, but during close mode, health checks run every 5 seconds with automated broker replacement. The additional cost of close-mode SLA is $1,500/month — only for the 144 hours when it is needed. The remaining 8,616 hours per year run at standard 99.95% SLA. This gives us 99.99% during close at 5% additional cost instead of 300% additional cost for year-round 99.99%."
adversarialResponse.pattern: tiered-response — accept the challenge, avoid defending the aggregate SLA, instead offer a time-boxed elevated SLA with specific mechanisms and costs. The Staff+ insight is that not all hours are equal — close-mode SLA targets the critical hours at minimal incremental cost.

sections.title: Exactly-Once Semantics & Data Integrity

description.value:
Exactly-once semantics (EOS) is the hardest guarantee to implement in a distributed integration hub. For SAP financial transactions, duplicate processing is unacceptable — you cannot post the same invoice twice. The integration hub must provide exactly-once delivery end-to-end, from SAP source to target application.

Kafka's Exactly-Once Semantics: Kafka 0.11+ provides exactly-once semantics within a Kafka cluster using transactional producers and idempotent producers. The producer assigns a unique transactional ID and sequence number to each message. The broker deduplicates messages with the same producer ID and sequence number. The consumer must be configured with isolation.level=read_committed to only read messages that are part of committed transactions. Kafka EOS works within a single Kafka cluster but does NOT provide exactly-once end-to-end — the source (SAP) and sink (target app) must each implement their own idempotency.

SAP Side Exactly-Once: On the SAP outbound side, exactly-once is achieved via Idempotent RFC + Transaction ID. Each SAP document has a unique transaction ID (LUW). When the integration hub receives an IDoc, it checks if this transaction ID has already been processed (stored in a deduplication table). If yes, it skips processing. If no, it processes and stores the transaction ID. The deduplication table has a TTL of 30 days (after which the transaction is considered safe to re-process if needed). This pattern handles the SAP side's at-least-once delivery (qRFC may retry) and ensures the hub processes each message exactly once.

Target Side Exactly-Once: On the consumer side (target application), exactly-once is achieved via Idempotent Consumers. The consumer stores the last processed offset per partition. When processing a message, the consumer first checks if this message's offset has already been processed. If yes, it skips. If no, it processes the message and commits the offset in the same transaction (using Kafka's transactional API with commitTransaction). This is the transactional outbox pattern: the consumer writes to its database and commits the Kafka offset atomically. If the consumer crashes between processing and committing, it re-processes the message on restart but the target application's idempotency check prevents duplicates.

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 400" 2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes pulse{0%,100%{opacity:.5}50%{opacity:1}}</style><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6 Z" commits offset</text><text class="eo-sub" x="185" y="196">May process duplicate if crash between</text><text class="eo-sub" x="185" y="212">process and commit — re-processing occurs</text><text class="eo-sub" x="185" y="228">OK for: operational data, notifications</text><rect class="eo-box" x="355" y="140" width="320" height="100" stroke="var(--svg-success-stroke)"/><text class="eo-lbl" x="515" y="160">Exactly-Once (higher guarantee)</text><text class="eo-sub" x="515" y="180">Consumer: process + commit in same txn</text><text class="eo-sub" x="515" y="196">Deduplication: check offset before process</text><text class="eo-sub" x="515" y="212">Idempotent: same message processed = no-op</text><text class="eo-sub" x="515" y="228">REQUIRED for: financial transactions</text><rect class="eo-box" x="25" y="260" width="650" height="90"/><text class="eo-lbl" x="350" y="280">Delivery Guarantees by Integration Type</text><text class="eo-sub" x="350" y="298">Financial postings (FI, CO): Exactly-Once — duplicate posting = audit failure</text><text class="eo-sub" x="350" y="314">Master data distribution: At-Least-Once with idempotent merge</text><text class="eo-sub" x="350" y="330">Notifications & alerts: At-Most-Once (loss acceptable over duplication)</text><text class="eo-sub" x="350" y="346">CDC streaming: Exactly-Once per partition (Kafka Connect + idempotent sinks)</text></svg>

sections.codeBlock:
" Idempotent consumer with offset dedup (pseudocode)
" Ensures exactly-once processing end-to-end

class IdempotentConsumer:
    def __init__(self):
        self.consumer = KafkaConsumer(
            "sap.sd.salesorder",
            group_id="order-processor",
            enable_auto_commit=False)
        self.db = PostgresConnection()

    def process(self):
        for msg in self.consumer:
            # Extract unique transaction ID from message
            tid = msg.value["id"]

            # Check dedup table (atomic INSERT ... ON CONFLICT)
            result = self.db.execute(
                "INSERT INTO processed_events (tid, status) "
                "VALUES (%s, 'PROCESSING') "
                "ON CONFLICT (tid) DO NOTHING "
                "RETURNING status",
                (tid,)
            )

            if result:
                # First time — process
                try:
                    self.apply_business_logic(msg.value)
                    self.db.execute(
                        "UPDATE processed_events SET status='DONE' "
                        "WHERE tid=%s", (tid,))
                    self.consumer.commit()
                except Exception:
                    self.db.execute(
                        "UPDATE processed_events SET status='FAILED' "
                        "WHERE tid=%s", (tid,))
                    # Move to DLQ after 3 retries
            else:
                # Duplicate — skip (idempotent replay)
                self.consumer.commit()
                continue

details: Staff+ Insight: The integration hub is a classic FAANG system design question for enterprise architects. The key differentiator is explicitly analyzing the Event Mesh vs Kafka vs Pulsar tradeoff with reasoning specific to SAP systems. Most candidates pick Kafka because it's popular. Staff+ candidates analyze the SAP integration depth, operational maturity, and total cost of ownership before making a recommendation. Always include the exactly-once semantics discussion — it's the hardest problem and where most designs fail.
